Use low-level disinfection for semicritical items to save processing time
rationale: Verify chemical indicator color change on every sterilized package before opening onto the sterile field This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for surgical site infections in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for wound classification during surgical site infections?
options: Omit chemical indicators from packages if biological indicators are used in the same load
Apply antiseptic skin preparation and immediately drape without allowing the solution to dry
Follow Standard Precautions for all patients regardless of known or suspected infectious status
Skip the Bowie-Dick test on days when the sterilizer seems to be functioning properly
rationale: Follow Standard Precautions for all patients regardless of known or suspected infectious status This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for surgical site infections in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for biological indicators during biological monitoring?
options: Store sterilized items in areas with high humidity levels exceeding 70%
Use razors for hair removal at the operative site the night before surgery
Classify all clean elective procedures as clean-contaminated for safety purposes
Use an integrator (Type 5 chemical indicator) as an internal indicator in every sterilized package
rationale: Use an integrator (Type 5 chemical indicator) as an internal indicator in every sterilized package This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for biological monitoring in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for chemical indicators during biological monitoring?
options: Perform the Bowie-Dick test daily in prevacuum steam sterilizers to verify air removal function
Use flash sterilization routinely to reduce instrument processing time between cases
Administer prophylactic antibiotics after the surgical incision is made
Use extended sterilization cycles to compensate for overloaded sterilizer chambers
rationale: Perform the Bowie-Dick test daily in prevacuum steam sterilizers to verify air removal function This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for biological monitoring in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for PPE requirements during standard precautions?
options: Skip biological indicator testing if chemical indicators consistently show proper color change
Maintain OR room temperature between 68-73°F (20-23°C) with humidity between 20-60%
Use low-level disinfection for semicritical items to save processing time
Omit chemical indicators from packages if biological indicators are used in the same load
rationale: Maintain OR room temperature between 68-73°F (20-23°C) with humidity between 20-60% This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for standard precautions in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for sharps safety during standard precautions?
options: Rely solely on visual inspection to determine if an item is sterile
Skip the Bowie-Dick test on days when the sterilizer seems to be functioning properly
Use immediate-use sterilization (flash sterilization) only for emergency situations, not for routine use
Store sterilized items in areas with high humidity levels exceeding 70%
rationale: Use immediate-use sterilization (flash sterilization) only for emergency situations, not for routine use This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for standard precautions in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for induction agents during general anesthesia?
options: Rely on pulse oximetry as the primary method for confirming endotracheal tube placement
Apply cricoid pressure during active vomiting to prevent aspiration
Wait for laboratory confirmation before beginning treatment for suspected malignant hyperthermia
Recognize the signs of malignant hyperthermia including rising end-tidal CO2, tachycardia, and muscle rigidity
rationale: Recognize the signs of malignant hyperthermia including rising end-tidal CO2, tachycardia, and muscle rigidity This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for general anesthesia in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for inhalation agents during general anesthesia?
options: Prepare dantrolene sodium immediately when malignant hyperthermia is suspected
Treat local anesthetic systemic toxicity with additional local anesthetic to counteract the reaction
Assume Stage II of anesthesia is the target stage for all surgical procedures
Use succinylcholine in patients with a known history of malignant hyperthermia
rationale: Prepare dantrolene sodium immediately when malignant hyperthermia is suspected This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for general anesthesia in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for spinal anesthesia during regional anesthesia?
options: Skip preparation of emergency airway equipment for routine cases under general anesthesia
Assist the anesthesia team with cricoid pressure during rapid sequence intubation when requested
Use the same size endotracheal tube for all adult patients regardless of anatomy
Allow the surgical technologist to administer medications from the anesthesia cart without authorization
rationale: Assist the anesthesia team with cricoid pressure during rapid sequence intubation when requested This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for regional anesthesia in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for epidural anesthesia during regional anesthesia?
options: Administer reversal agents as a routine practice at the end of every surgical procedure
Delay vasopressor administration during spinal-induced hypotension until the blood pressure drops below 70 systolic
Ensure the anesthesia cart includes emergency airway equipment and resuscitation medications
Rely on pulse oximetry as the primary method for confirming endotracheal tube placement
rationale: Ensure the anesthesia cart includes emergency airway equipment and resuscitation medications This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for regional anesthesia in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for sedation levels during monitored anesthesia care?
options: Store dantrolene at the pharmacy rather than keeping it readily available in the OR suite
Wait for laboratory confirmation before beginning treatment for suspected malignant hyperthermia
Treat local anesthetic systemic toxicity with additional local anesthetic to counteract the reaction
Monitor the patient for signs of local anesthetic systemic toxicity during regional anesthesia procedures
rationale: Monitor the patient for signs of local anesthetic systemic toxicity during regional anesthesia procedures This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for monitored anesthesia care in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for monitoring requirements during monitored anesthesia care?
options: Recognize that Stage III is the desired stage of general anesthesia for surgical procedures
Apply cricoid pressure during active vomiting to prevent aspiration
Use succinylcholine in patients with a known history of malignant hyperthermia
Skip preparation of emergency airway equipment for routine cases under general anesthesia
rationale: Recognize that Stage III is the desired stage of general anesthesia for surgical procedures This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for monitored anesthesia care in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for anesthesia machine during anesthesia equipment?
options: Assume Stage II of anesthesia is the target stage for all surgical procedures
Keep intralipid readily available for the treatment of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST)
Allow the surgical technologist to administer medications from the anesthesia cart without authorization
Administer reversal agents as a routine practice at the end of every surgical procedure
rationale: Keep intralipid readily available for the treatment of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for anesthesia equipment in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for endotracheal tubes during anesthesia equipment?
options: Use the same size endotracheal tube for all adult patients regardless of anatomy
Rely on pulse oximetry as the primary method for confirming endotracheal tube placement
Identify succinylcholine as the depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agent used for rapid intubation
Store dantrolene at the pharmacy rather than keeping it readily available in the OR suite
rationale: Identify succinylcholine as the depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agent used for rapid intubation This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for anesthesia equipment in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for opioids during pharmacology?
options: Delay vasopressor administration during spinal-induced hypotension until the blood pressure drops below 70 systolic
Treat local anesthetic systemic toxicity with additional local anesthetic to counteract the reaction
Apply cricoid pressure during active vomiting to prevent aspiration
Assist with airway management by having backup airway devices available including LMA and bougie
rationale: Assist with airway management by having backup airway devices available including LMA and bougie This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for pharmacology in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for benzodiazepines during pharmacology?
options: Verify the availability of reversal agents including naloxone, flumazenil, and neostigmine/glycopyrrolate
Wait for laboratory confirmation before beginning treatment for suspected malignant hyperthermia
Skip preparation of emergency airway equipment for routine cases under general anesthesia
Assume Stage II of anesthesia is the target stage for all surgical procedures
rationale: Verify the availability of reversal agents including naloxone, flumazenil, and neostigmine/glycopyrrolate This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for pharmacology in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for intraoperative bleeding during hemorrhage management?
options: Cover evisceration with dry sterile gauze and return the organs to the abdominal cavity
Prepare for emergency conversion from laparoscopic to open surgery if intraoperative hemorrhage is uncontrolled
Apply a tourniquet above the surgical site for all cases of intraoperative hemorrhage
Postpone documentation of complications until the postoperative report
rationale: Prepare for emergency conversion from laparoscopic to open surgery if intraoperative hemorrhage is uncontrolled This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for hemorrhage management in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for hemostatic agents during hemorrhage management?
options: Wait for vital sign changes before treating suspected malignant hyperthermia
Skip prophylactic DVT measures for procedures lasting less than one hour
Use topical hemostatic agents such as Gelfoam, Surgicel, or thrombin as directed by the surgeon
Ignore a slightly elevated temperature during surgery if the patient is otherwise stable
rationale: Use topical hemostatic agents such as Gelfoam, Surgicel, or thrombin as directed by the surgeon This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for hemorrhage management in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for dehiscence during wound complications?
options: Use water to extinguish a surgical fire on or near the patient
Handle a fire involving the airway by increasing oxygen flow to maintain ventilation
Continue the procedure and monitor the bleeding without immediate intervention
Recognize wound dehiscence risk factors including obesity, diabetes, malnutrition, and steroid use
rationale: Recognize wound dehiscence risk factors including obesity, diabetes, malnutrition, and steroid use This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for wound complications in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for evisceration during wound complications?
options: Implement surgical fire prevention by managing fuel, oxidizer, and ignition sources proactively
Close the wound over a suspected retained foreign body and follow up with postoperative imaging
Rely solely on the surgeon to identify and manage all intraoperative complications
Cover evisceration with dry sterile gauze and return the organs to the abdominal cavity
rationale: Implement surgical fire prevention by managing fuel, oxidizer, and ignition sources proactively This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for wound complications in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for malignant hyperthermia during anesthesia complications?
options: Defer massive transfusion protocol activation until the patient shows signs of hemodynamic instability
Activate the code blue protocol immediately when cardiac arrest is identified in the operating room
Postpone documentation of complications until the postoperative report
Wait for vital sign changes before treating suspected malignant hyperthermia
rationale: Activate the code blue protocol immediately when cardiac arrest is identified in the operating room This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for anesthesia complications in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for laryngospasm during anesthesia complications?
options: Apply a tourniquet above the surgical site for all cases of intraoperative hemorrhage
Ignore a slightly elevated temperature during surgery if the patient is otherwise stable
Apply direct pressure and call for hemostatic agents when intraoperative hemorrhage is identified
Use water to extinguish a surgical fire on or near the patient
rationale: Apply direct pressure and call for hemostatic agents when intraoperative hemorrhage is identified This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for anesthesia complications in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for nerve damage during positioning injuries?
options: Skip prophylactic DVT measures for procedures lasting less than one hour
Continue the procedure and monitor the bleeding without immediate intervention
Close the wound over a suspected retained foreign body and follow up with postoperative imaging
Recognize evisceration as a surgical emergency requiring immediate coverage with warm saline-moistened sterile towels
rationale: Recognize evisceration as a surgical emergency requiring immediate coverage with warm saline-moistened sterile towels This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for positioning injuries in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for compartment syndrome during positioning injuries?
options: Prepare ice and a cooling blanket when malignant hyperthermia is suspected during surgery
Handle a fire involving the airway by increasing oxygen flow to maintain ventilation
Cover evisceration with dry sterile gauze and return the organs to the abdominal cavity
Defer massive transfusion protocol activation until the patient shows signs of hemodynamic instability
rationale: Prepare ice and a cooling blanket when malignant hyperthermia is suspected during surgery This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for positioning injuries in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for cardiac arrest in OR during emergency situations?
options: Rely solely on the surgeon to identify and manage all intraoperative complications
Identify increasing end-tidal CO2 and muscle rigidity as early signs of malignant hyperthermia
Wait for vital sign changes before treating suspected malignant hyperthermia
Apply a tourniquet above the surgical site for all cases of intraoperative hemorrhage
rationale: Identify increasing end-tidal CO2 and muscle rigidity as early signs of malignant hyperthermia This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for emergency situations in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to Canadian CSTA standards and CSA guidelines, what is the established requirement for fire safety during emergency situations?
options: Postpone documentation of complications until the postoperative report
Use water to extinguish a surgical fire on or near the patient
Stop the procedure and apply the RACE protocol when a surgical fire occurs in the operating room
Skip prophylactic DVT measures for procedures lasting less than one hour
rationale: Stop the procedure and apply the RACE protocol when a surgical fire occurs in the operating room This reflects Canadian CSTA and CSA standards for emergency situations in surgical technology practice.

stem: According to AORN guidelines, when should the surgical technologist re-scrub during a procedure?
options: When glove integrity is compromised or after a contamination event, following the same scrub protocol
Only at the beginning of each new case
Every 2 hours regardless of glove status
Re-scrubbing is never required once the initial scrub is performed
rationale: AORN requires re-scrubbing when sterile barrier integrity is compromised or after contamination events.

stem: Per AORN guidelines, when is the open gloving technique acceptable for the surgical technologist?
options: Only when performing the second gloving after a glove change during a procedure
As the primary gloving method for all procedures
Whenever the closed technique is too time-consuming
Open gloving is never acceptable for the scrub role
rationale: AORN permits open gloving for the scrub role only during glove changes, not as the initial gloving method.

stem: Under AORN standards, who is responsible for initiating the surgical count?
options: The circulating nurse and the surgical technologist together
Only the surgeon
The charge nurse before the case starts
Any available OR staff member
rationale: AORN designates the circulating nurse and surgical technologist as jointly responsible for initiating and performing surgical counts.

stem: Per US CAP (College of American Pathologists) standards, what fixative is standard for most surgical tissue specimens?
options: 10% neutral buffered formalin
Normal saline solution
Sterile water
Alcohol-based fixative
rationale: CAP standards specify 10% neutral buffered formalin as the standard fixative for most surgical tissue specimens.

stem: According to NBSTSA content, which scissors are specifically designed for cutting sutures?
options: Straight Mayo scissors
Metzenbaum scissors
Iris scissors
Bandage scissors
rationale: Straight Mayo scissors are the standard instrument for cutting sutures according to NBSTSA surgical technology content.

stem: Per US surgical technology standards, what is the primary difference between tissue forceps with teeth and smooth forceps?
options: Toothed forceps provide a more secure grip on dense tissue while smooth forceps are used for delicate tissue handling
Smooth forceps are only used for skin closure
Toothed forceps are used exclusively for vessel occlusion
There is no functional difference between the two types
rationale: Toothed forceps provide superior grip on dense tissue while smooth forceps minimize tissue damage on delicate structures.

stem: According to US SAGES (Society of American Gastrointestinal and Endoscopic Surgeons) guidelines, what is the critical safety step before establishing pneumoperitoneum during laparoscopy?
options: Verify proper Veress needle placement by performing the drop test and confirming low opening pressure
Skip verification if the patient has had prior laparoscopic surgery
Use maximum insufflation pressure to establish pneumoperitoneum quickly
Rely solely on visual confirmation through the camera
rationale: SAGES emphasizes verification of proper Veress needle placement through the drop test and opening pressure confirmation before insufflation.

stem: Per US AUA (American Urological Association) standards, what irrigation solution is used during TURP to prevent TUR syndrome?
options: Non-electrolyte hypotonic solutions such as glycine 1.5% or sorbitol-mannitol with careful monitoring of absorption
Normal saline in all cases
Sterile water to reduce bacterial contamination
Lactated Ringer's solution for electrolyte balance
rationale: AUA standards specify non-electrolyte solutions like glycine or sorbitol-mannitol for TURP with careful monitoring of fluid absorption to prevent TUR syndrome.

stem: According to AORN positioning standards, what is the maximum recommended Trendelenburg angle for most surgical procedures?
options: Generally not to exceed 30 degrees unless surgically necessary, with continuous hemodynamic monitoring
45 degrees is standard for all abdominal procedures
No maximum angle is specified by AORN
10 degrees for all procedures regardless of surgical need
rationale: AORN generally recommends Trendelenburg angles not exceed 30 degrees unless surgically necessary, with continuous hemodynamic monitoring.

stem: According to US OSHA standards, what is the required minimum PPE for all surgical team members in the sterile field?
options: Surgical cap, mask, eye protection, sterile gown, and sterile gloves
Mask and gloves only
Gloves and gown without eye protection
PPE requirements vary by procedure type only
rationale: OSHA and AORN require complete PPE including cap, mask, eye protection, sterile gown, and gloves for all personnel in the sterile field.

stem: Per US FDA labeling requirements, which reversal agent is used to reverse the effects of benzodiazepines?
options: Flumazenil (Romazicon)
Naloxone (Narcan)
Neostigmine
Sugammadex (Bridion)
rationale: Flumazenil is the FDA-approved specific reversal agent for benzodiazepine effects.

stem: According to ASRA (American Society of Regional Anesthesia) guidelines, what is the maximum recommended dose of bupivacaine for peripheral nerve blocks?
options: 2.5 mg/kg without epinephrine and 3 mg/kg with epinephrine, not to exceed 175-225 mg total
No maximum dose is established for bupivacaine
10 mg/kg regardless of epinephrine use
The same dose as lidocaine (4.5 mg/kg)
rationale: ASRA guidelines specify maximum bupivacaine doses of 2.5 mg/kg (without epi) and 3 mg/kg (with epi) to prevent systemic toxicity.

stem: Per MHAUS (Malignant Hyperthermia Association of the United States) protocol, what is the initial dantrolene dose and reconstitution requirement?
options: 2.5 mg/kg IV with each vial requiring 60 mL of sterile water for injection, administered rapidly
1 mg/kg mixed with normal saline
5 mg/kg dissolved in dextrose solution
0.5 mg/kg administered intramuscularly
rationale: MHAUS protocol specifies initial dantrolene at 2.5 mg/kg, with each vial reconstituted in 60 mL of sterile water for injection.

stem: According to US NFPA 99 (National Fire Protection Association) Health Care Facilities Code, what type of fire extinguisher should be available in the operating room?
options: Carbon dioxide (CO2) extinguisher for use near electrical equipment and a water mist type for non-electrical fires
Only ABC dry chemical extinguishers
Halon extinguishers exclusively
Fire extinguishers are not required inside the OR suite
rationale: NFPA 99 recommends CO2 extinguishers for electrical equipment areas and water mist for non-electrical fires in the OR.

stem: According to CSA Z314.0 Canadian standards, what is the correct method for opening wrapped sterile supplies onto the sterile field?
options: Open the furthest flap first, then the side flaps, and the nearest flap last, without reaching over the sterile contents
Open all flaps simultaneously for efficiency
The circulating nurse should place items directly on the sterile field
Opening sequence does not matter as long as the contents remain dry
rationale: CSA Z314.0 specifies the sequential opening method—far flap first, sides, then near flap—to prevent contamination from reaching over sterile contents.

stem: Under CSTA standards, what is the maximum height at which sterile items should be maintained on the sterile field?
options: Between waist and shoulder level of the scrubbed team member
At any height as long as the item is wrapped
Below the waist for stability
Above shoulder level for visibility
rationale: CSTA standards specify that sterile items must be maintained between waist and shoulder level to remain within the designated sterile zone.

stem: According to CSA Z317.2 Canadian healthcare facility standards, what are the air quality requirements for operating rooms?
options: Minimum 20 air changes per hour with at least 4 fresh air changes, HEPA filtration, and positive pressure relative to corridors
10 air changes per hour with standard filtration
No specific air quality requirements for Canadian ORs
The same air quality as general patient care areas
rationale: CSA Z317.2 requires 20 air changes per hour with minimum 4 fresh air changes, HEPA filtration, and positive pressure in Canadian ORs.

stem: According to the Canadian Patient Safety Institute, what is the required debriefing element unique to the Canadian Surgical Safety Checklist?
options: A structured post-procedure review including equipment issues, specimen confirmation, and key concerns for recovery
Debriefing is optional in Canadian surgical practice
Only the surgeon participates in the debriefing
Debriefing occurs only if a complication arose during surgery
rationale: The Canadian Patient Safety Institute mandates structured post-procedure debriefing covering equipment, specimens, and recovery concerns as part of the safety checklist.

stem: Under Health Canada Medical Device Regulations, what is required before a new surgical instrument can be used in a Canadian operating room?
options: The device must have a valid Health Canada medical device license, be listed in MDALL, and comply with applicable CSA standards
Only the manufacturer's CE marking is required
Instruments from any country can be used without Canadian regulatory approval
Provincial health authorities individually approve each instrument
rationale: Health Canada requires a valid medical device license and MDALL listing before surgical instruments can be used in Canadian facilities.

stem: According to Canadian Neurosurgical Society standards, what specific preparation is required for the surgical technologist during a craniotomy?
options: Prepare craniotomy instruments including perforator and craniotome, bone wax, bipolar cautery with irrigation, cottonoid patties, and hemostatic agents per CNS guidelines
Standard general surgery instruments are sufficient for craniotomy
Only the surgeon needs to verify neurosurgery-specific instruments
Craniotomy preparation follows the same protocol as any orthopedic procedure
rationale: CNS standards require specific craniotomy preparation including perforator, craniotome, bone wax, bipolar cautery, cottonoids, and hemostatic agents.

stem: According to Canadian perioperative nursing standards (ORNAC), what is the required protocol when placing a patient in lithotomy position?
options: Two team members must raise the legs simultaneously, padding all contact points, with stirrup height adjusted to avoid hip flexion beyond 90 degrees
One team member can manage both legs if experienced
Stirrup adjustment is at the surgeon's discretion without specific angle limits
Lithotomy position does not require additional padding in Canadian standards
rationale: ORNAC standards require simultaneous bilateral leg elevation by two team members with proper padding and hip flexion limits.

stem: According to CSA Z314.3 standards, what is the required documentation for each sterilization cycle in Canadian healthcare facilities?
options: Load contents, cycle parameters, operator identification, chemical and biological indicator results, and date of sterilization
Only the date and time of the cycle
Documentation is required only for implant loads
Electronic records alone without physical documentation
rationale: CSA Z314.3 mandates comprehensive documentation including load contents, parameters, operator ID, indicator results, and date for every sterilization cycle.

stem: Under Canadian CSA Z314.3 standards, what is the required action when a biological indicator test result is positive?
options: Immediately recall all items from the suspect load, take the sterilizer out of service, investigate the cause, and notify infection prevention and control
Rerun the cycle and use the items if the second test is negative
Continue using items from the load while investigating
Report the positive result at the next quality meeting
rationale: CSA Z314.3 requires immediate recall of suspect load items, sterilizer removal from service, root cause investigation, and infection control notification on positive BI results.

stem: According to CAS (Canadian Anesthesiologists' Society) guidelines, what level of monitoring is required during monitored anesthesia care (MAC)?
options: The same standard of monitoring as general anesthesia including pulse oximetry, capnography when feasible, ECG, and blood pressure per CAS standards
Only pulse oximetry and periodic blood pressure checks
Monitoring requirements are reduced for MAC compared to general anesthesia
Capnography is never required during MAC procedures
rationale: CAS guidelines require the same monitoring standard for MAC as general anesthesia, including oximetry, capnography when feasible, ECG, and blood pressure.

stem: Under Canadian anesthesia practice standards, what is the required availability of a difficult airway cart?
options: A difficult airway cart must be immediately available in every operating room suite with contents checked and restocked regularly per CAS standards
A single cart shared among all ORs on the same floor is sufficient
Difficult airway equipment is only required for ENT cases
The anesthesiologist carries their own airway equipment
rationale: CAS standards mandate immediate availability of a difficult airway cart in every OR suite with regular inventory checks.

stem: According to Canadian Accreditation Standards, what is the required protocol for managing surgical wound dehiscence?
options: Cover the wound with warm saline-moistened sterile dressings, place the patient in low Fowler's position, prepare for emergency return to OR, and notify the surgeon immediately
Apply dry dressings and reassess in 24 hours
Document the dehiscence and continue routine care
Apply adhesive strips to approximate wound edges
rationale: Canadian accreditation standards require immediate wound coverage with moist sterile dressings, positioning, OR preparation, and surgeon notification for dehiscence.

stem: Under Canadian provincial workers' compensation standards, what documentation is required when a positioning-related injury is identified postoperatively?
options: An incident report including positioning method, duration, padding used, personnel involved, and postoperative assessment findings per provincial OH&S requirements
Only a note in the patient's chart
Documentation is only required if the patient files a complaint
The surgeon's operative note is sufficient documentation
rationale: Canadian provincial OH&S standards require comprehensive incident reporting for positioning injuries including all positioning details and assessment findings.

stem: What is the first step when beginning the surgical hand scrub at the scrub sink?
options: Open the scrub brush package and adjust water temperature before wetting hands and forearms
Apply the scrub agent before wetting the hands
Begin scrubbing the forearms before the hands
Put on sterile gloves before approaching the scrub sink
rationale: The first step is to open supplies and adjust water, then wet hands and forearms before applying the scrub agent.

stem: After completing the surgical hand scrub, what is the correct method for drying the hands?
options: Use a sterile towel from the gown pack, drying each hand and forearm with a separate area of the towel
Use the gown itself to dry the hands
Air dry the hands while waiting for the gown
Use paper towels from the wall dispenser
rationale: Hands must be dried with a sterile towel from the gown pack, using a separate area for each hand and forearm to maintain asepsis.

stem: When setting up the back table, in what order should sterile supplies be arranged?
options: Organize by category and anticipated order of use, with the most frequently needed items most accessible
Place items randomly for quick access to any instrument
Arrange alphabetically by instrument name
Stack all instruments in a single pile to save space
rationale: Back table organization by category and anticipated order of use ensures efficient instrument access during the procedure.

stem: Who must be present and actively participating during the surgical timeout?
options: All members of the surgical team including surgeon, anesthesiologist, surgical technologist, and circulating nurse
Only the surgeon and anesthesiologist
Any two members of the surgical team
The circulating nurse alone
rationale: The surgical timeout requires active participation from all surgical team members to verify patient safety information.

stem: What items must be included in the initial surgical count?
options: Sponges, sharps, instruments, and miscellaneous items such as vessel loops and bulldogs
Only sponges and instruments
Only sponges and sharps
Items are counted only if the surgeon requests it
rationale: Initial surgical counts must include sponges, sharps, instruments, and all miscellaneous countable items to prevent retention.

stem: What is the difference between Mayo scissors and Metzenbaum scissors in surgical use?
options: Mayo scissors are heavier and designed for cutting tough tissue and sutures, while Metzenbaum scissors are lighter for delicate tissue dissection
There is no significant difference between the two
Metzenbaum scissors are stronger and used for cutting bone
Mayo scissors are exclusively used for skin incisions
rationale: Mayo scissors are designed for cutting heavier tissue and sutures, while Metzenbaum scissors are designed for delicate tissue dissection near vital structures.

stem: What is the primary difference between a Kelly clamp and a mosquito clamp?
options: A Kelly clamp is larger with serrations extending halfway along the jaws, while a mosquito clamp is smaller with full serrations for fine vessels
They are identical instruments with different names
Mosquito clamps are used only for orthopedic surgery
Kelly clamps have teeth while mosquito clamps are smooth
rationale: Kelly clamps are larger with partial jaw serrations for medium vessels, while mosquito clamps are smaller with full serrations for fine vessel hemostasis.

stem: What is the surgical technologist's primary responsibility during the skin incision phase of a procedure?
options: Have the appropriate knife loaded, suction ready, electrosurgery available, and be prepared to provide hemostatic instruments
Stand back and observe the surgeon's technique
Focus only on documentation during this phase
Prepare closure materials during the incision
rationale: During skin incision, the ST must have the knife, suction, ESU, and hemostatic instruments immediately available to support the surgeon.

stem: What special equipment consideration is essential when preparing for an orthopedic procedure involving implants?
options: Verify implant availability, check sizes with the surgeon, maintain implant sterility, and have backup sizes available
Implants are managed entirely by the sales representative
Standard general surgery instruments are sufficient for all orthopedic cases
Implant verification can be done after the incision
rationale: Implant preparation requires verification, size confirmation with the surgeon, sterility maintenance, and backup size availability before the procedure begins.

stem: What is the standard safety strap placement for a patient in the supine position?
options: Across the thighs approximately 2 inches above the knees, snug but not restrictive to circulation
Across the patient's chest
Directly over the kneecaps for maximum security
No safety strap is needed in the supine position
rationale: The safety strap should be placed across the thighs approximately 2 inches above the knees, snug enough to secure the patient without restricting circulation.

stem: What complication is the surgical technologist most vigilant about when a patient is positioned prone?
options: Increased intraocular pressure leading to postoperative vision loss, requiring proper head positioning and padding
Hypertension from the prone position
Improved venous return causing fluid overload
Decreased risk of airway complications
rationale: Prone positioning increases intraocular pressure risk, which can lead to postoperative vision loss—requiring vigilant head positioning and eye protection.

stem: According to the CDC wound classification system, which class describes a wound that enters the respiratory, alimentary, or genitourinary tract under controlled conditions?
options: Class II: Clean-contaminated
Class I: Clean
Class III: Contaminated
Class IV: Dirty-infected
rationale: Class II (clean-contaminated) wounds involve controlled entry into the respiratory, alimentary, or genitourinary tract without unusual contamination.

stem: According to the Spaulding classification, which category requires sterilization rather than disinfection?
options: Critical items that enter sterile tissue or the vascular system, such as surgical instruments and implants
Semicritical items that contact mucous membranes
Noncritical items that contact intact skin
All items used in the operating room regardless of contact type
rationale: The Spaulding classification categorizes items entering sterile tissue or vasculature as critical, requiring sterilization.

stem: What are the four stages of general anesthesia that the surgical technologist should understand?
options: Stage I (analgesia), Stage II (excitement/delirium), Stage III (surgical anesthesia), and Stage IV (medullary depression/overdose)
Induction, maintenance, emergence, and recovery
Light, moderate, deep, and general
Pre-anesthetic, anesthetic, surgical, and post-anesthetic
rationale: The four classical stages are Stage I (analgesia), Stage II (excitement), Stage III (surgical anesthesia—the target), and Stage IV (overdose/medullary depression).

stem: What is the primary purpose of the pulse oximeter in the operating room?
options: To continuously monitor the patient's oxygen saturation (SpO2) and pulse rate
To measure blood pressure
To monitor body temperature
To assess depth of anesthesia
rationale: The pulse oximeter continuously measures oxygen saturation and pulse rate, providing real-time assessment of the patient's oxygenation status.

stem: What are the most common topical hemostatic agents used in the operating room?
options: Gelfoam (absorbable gelatin), Surgicel (oxidized cellulose), bone wax, and thrombin
Only electrocautery is used for hemostasis
Topical antibiotics serve as hemostatic agents
Saline irrigation is the primary hemostatic method
rationale: Common topical hemostatic agents include Gelfoam, Surgicel, bone wax, and thrombin, each with specific indications and mechanisms.

stem: What is the difference between wound dehiscence and wound evisceration?
options: Dehiscence is separation of wound layers while evisceration involves protrusion of organs through the separated wound
They are the same complication with different names
Evisceration is a minor complication while dehiscence is an emergency
Dehiscence only occurs in skin while evisceration occurs in muscle
rationale: Dehiscence is the separation of wound layers, while evisceration is the more severe protrusion of organs through the open wound, requiring emergency intervention.

stem: What is the RACE acronym used for in operating room fire emergencies?
options: Rescue the patient, Activate the alarm, Contain the fire, Extinguish or Evacuate
Run, Alert, Call, Escape
Report, Assess, Control, Evaluate
Remove, Apply, Check, Exit
rationale: RACE stands for Rescue, Activate (alarm), Contain (fire), and Extinguish/Evacuate—the standardized fire response protocol.

stem: A sterile package is found with moisture staining on the outer wrapper but the inner wrapper appears intact. What is the correct action?
options: Consider the package contaminated due to potential strike-through and do not use it
Use the inner contents if they appear dry
Dry the outer wrapper and use the package
Open the package and inspect the contents before deciding
rationale: Moisture on sterile packaging creates strike-through contamination potential, making the entire package unsuitable for use regardless of inner wrapper appearance.

stem: Why is positive air pressure maintained in the operating room relative to the corridor?
options: To prevent contaminated air from flowing into the OR from less clean adjacent areas
To help circulate anesthetic gases more efficiently
To keep the room temperature more stable
Positive pressure is not actually required in modern ORs
rationale: Positive pressure in the OR prevents contaminated corridor air from flowing into the surgical environment, reducing airborne contamination risk.

stem: During a thyroidectomy, which retractor is most commonly used to provide exposure of the thyroid gland?
options: Green (thyroid) retractor or Lahey retractor
Balfour retractor
Finochietto retractor
Bookwalter retractor
rationale: The Green thyroid retractor and Lahey retractor are specifically designed for thyroid surgery, providing appropriate exposure of the thyroid gland and surrounding structures.

stem: What specialized instruments must be available on the sterile field for a total abdominal hysterectomy?
options: Long instruments including Heaney clamps, uterine manipulator, long curved scissors, long needle holders, and Deaver retractors
Standard general surgery instruments are sufficient
Only laparoscopic instruments are needed
Orthopedic instruments for pelvic bone access
rationale: Total abdominal hysterectomy requires long pelvic instruments including Heaney clamps, uterine manipulator, long scissors, and appropriate retractors for deep pelvic access.

stem: What is the purpose of the axillary roll when a patient is in the lateral position?
options: To prevent compression of the dependent axillary neurovascular bundle and allow chest expansion
To provide comfort for the patient's arm
To elevate the head above the heart
To stabilize the patient's hip position
rationale: The axillary roll, placed caudal to the axilla, prevents compression of the dependent axillary neurovascular structures and facilitates chest expansion.

stem: When should surgical hand hygiene be performed by the surgical technologist?
options: Before every surgical procedure, before regowning/regloving, and whenever moving between sterile tasks
Only before the first case of the day
Only when the hands are visibly soiled
Hand hygiene is only required of the surgeon
rationale: Surgical hand hygiene is required before every procedure, before regowning, and whenever transitioning between sterile tasks.

stem: What reversal agent is used specifically to reverse the effects of non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents?
options: Neostigmine combined with glycopyrrolate or atropine, or sugammadex for aminosteroid agents
Naloxone (Narcan)
Flumazenil (Romazicon)
Epinephrine
rationale: Neostigmine (with anticholinergic) or sugammadex specifically reverses non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockade.

stem: Which nerve is most commonly injured during surgical positioning in the supine position?
options: Ulnar nerve at the elbow due to compression against the medial epicondyle
Femoral nerve
Sciatic nerve
Radial nerve
rationale: The ulnar nerve at the medial epicondyle of the elbow is the most commonly injured nerve in supine positioning due to compression against the OR table or armboard.

stem: How many strokes per surface are recommended in the counted stroke scrub method?
options: 30 strokes per surface of each finger, hand, and arm
10 strokes per surface
50 strokes per surface
Strokes are not counted in any standard method
rationale: The counted stroke method specifies 30 strokes per surface area for adequate microbial reduction.

stem: What should the surgical technologist do if the scrub brush is dropped during the surgical hand scrub?
options: Discard the brush, obtain a new one, and restart the scrub
Rinse the brush and continue scrubbing
Complete the scrub without the brush
Use the dropped brush for the arms only
rationale: A dropped scrub brush is contaminated and must be replaced, with the scrub restarted.

stem: When a new sponge package is opened during a procedure, what must the surgical technologist do?
options: Count the sponges with the circulating nurse and add them to the count record before placing them on the sterile field
Place them on the field and count later
Only count them during the closing count
The circulating nurse counts independently
rationale: All sponges added during a procedure must be counted collaboratively and recorded before use on the sterile field.

stem: What type of sponges should be used in deep body cavities to facilitate counting and detection?
options: Radiopaque (X-ray detectable) laparotomy sponges with a radiopaque strip or marker
Small 4x4 gauze sponges
Cotton balls wrapped in gauze
Non-radiopaque sponges if they are brightly colored
rationale: Radiopaque laparotomy sponges are required for deep cavities because they are detectable on X-ray if retained.

stem: What is the proper technique for passing a loaded needle holder to the surgeon?
options: Present it handle-first with the needle pointed away from both individuals, with the suture trailing and visible
Pass it with the needle pointed toward the surgeon
Toss it onto the sterile field near the surgeon
Hand it to the circulating nurse to pass to the surgeon
rationale: Loaded needle holders should be passed handle-first with the needle pointed safely away and suture trailing for safe transfer.

stem: Which tissue forceps should be selected when handling bowel tissue to minimize trauma?
options: Smooth (non-toothed) forceps such as DeBakey or smooth Adson forceps
Heavy toothed forceps for a secure grip
Kocher forceps with teeth
Allis tissue clamps
rationale: Smooth, non-toothed forceps minimize tissue trauma when handling delicate bowel tissue.

stem: During a laparoscopic procedure, what is the surgical technologist's responsibility regarding the camera system?
options: Ensure the camera is white-balanced, focused, anti-fogged, and maintain a clear operative view by cleaning the lens as needed
The camera system is entirely managed by the surgeon
Camera setup is only done by biomedical engineering
The camera does not require adjustment once set up
rationale: The ST must ensure the laparoscopic camera is properly white-balanced, focused, anti-fogged, and kept clean for optimal visualization.

stem: What is the correct technique for handling a tissue specimen removed during surgery?
options: Receive the specimen in a basin, keep it moist, label it with the surgeon's identification of the tissue and site, and pass it to the circulator for formal labeling and transport
Air-dry the specimen for better preservation
Place all specimens in a single container regardless of site
Label specimens after the procedure is complete
rationale: Specimens should be received in a basin, kept moist, identified by the surgeon, and properly labeled for accurate pathological analysis.

stem: What modifications to the supine position are commonly used for laparoscopic abdominal procedures?
options: Trendelenburg or reverse Trendelenburg with arms secured at the sides to accommodate laparoscopic tower and team positioning
Supine with arms extended on arm boards
No modifications are ever needed for laparoscopic cases
Lateral tilt is the only modification used
rationale: Laparoscopic procedures often require Trendelenburg/reverse Trendelenburg modifications with arms tucked to allow proper equipment and team positioning.

stem: What is the primary advantage of hydrogen peroxide plasma sterilization over ethylene oxide?
options: Shorter cycle time with no toxic residuals, making it safer for staff and environment
It can sterilize heat-stable items at higher temperatures
It is effective on all types of materials including cellulose
It is less expensive than all other sterilization methods
rationale: Hydrogen peroxide plasma sterilization offers shorter cycle times without toxic residuals, unlike EtO which requires aeration time for toxic gas removal.

stem: What material limitation prevents the use of hydrogen peroxide plasma sterilization for certain items?
options: It cannot be used for cellulose-based materials (paper, linen) or items with long narrow lumens that restrict vapor penetration
It damages all metal instruments
It cannot sterilize plastic items
There are no material limitations for this method
rationale: Hydrogen peroxide plasma cannot penetrate cellulose materials or long narrow lumens, limiting its use for certain items.

stem: What are the early warning signs of malignant hyperthermia that the surgical technologist should recognize?
options: Unexplained increase in end-tidal CO2, tachycardia, muscle rigidity (especially masseter spasm), and rising body temperature
Only a sudden drop in blood pressure
Bradycardia and hypothermia
Decreased oxygen saturation without other symptoms
rationale: Early MH signs include rising ETCO2, tachycardia, muscle rigidity (especially masseter), and temperature elevation—the ST must recognize these to assist in rapid response.

stem: In the event of a power failure during a surgical procedure, what is the surgical technologist's immediate priority?
options: Protect the sterile field, confirm backup power activation, ensure critical equipment is on emergency power, and maintain communication with the team
Leave the sterile field to check the power panel
Continue the procedure exactly as planned
Evacuate the operating room immediately
rationale: During power failure, the ST must protect the sterile field, verify backup power, ensure critical equipment function, and maintain team communication.

stem: What are the immediate steps if an uncontrolled arterial hemorrhage occurs during surgery?
options: Apply direct pressure, alert the surgeon, prepare additional hemostatic instruments and agents, and have blood products available per massive transfusion protocol
Apply a tourniquet regardless of the surgical site
Wait for the surgeon to identify the source before taking action
Irrigate the site with cold saline only
rationale: Uncontrolled arterial hemorrhage requires immediate direct pressure, surgeon notification, hemostatic preparation, and blood product availability.

stem: If a surgical technologist's gown sleeve touches a non-sterile surface during a procedure, what must be done?
options: The gown is considered contaminated; the surgical technologist must regown and reglove with assistance from the circulating nurse
Wipe the sleeve with antiseptic and continue
Fold the contaminated portion inward and continue
Only reglove without changing the gown
rationale: Contact with a non-sterile surface contaminates the gown, requiring complete regowning and regloving.

stem: What is the principle of 'event-related sterility' and how does it apply to stored surgical supplies?
options: Items remain sterile until a specific event compromises packaging integrity, regardless of the date of sterilization
Items are sterile only for a specific number of days based on packaging type
All items must be resterilized every 30 days
Event-related sterility only applies to implantable devices
rationale: Event-related sterility means items remain sterile until a contaminating event occurs, replacing time-based expiration with packaging integrity assessment.

stem: Why is it important to never add formalin to a specimen container that is intended for a frozen section analysis?
options: Formalin fixation prevents the tissue from being properly frozen and sectioned for rapid pathological interpretation
Formalin enhances the frozen section process
Formalin is only used for permanent sections of the same tissue
There is no distinction in handling between frozen and permanent sections
rationale: Formalin-fixed tissue cannot be properly processed for frozen sections, which require fresh tissue for rapid interpretation.

stem: What is the purpose of the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist in the operating room?
options: To reduce surgical complications and mortality by ensuring critical safety steps are performed before, during, and after surgery
To speed up the surgical procedure
To document billing information for the case
To satisfy insurance requirements only
rationale: The WHO Surgical Safety Checklist aims to reduce surgical complications and mortality through systematic verification of critical safety steps.

stem: What instruments are specifically required for a laparoscopic instrument set that differ from open surgery instruments?
options: Trocar and sleeve assemblies, laparoscopic graspers, scissors, clip appliers, electrosurgical hooks, and a camera/light cord system
Standard open surgery instruments in longer lengths
Only a camera and light source are needed in addition to standard instruments
Laparoscopic procedures use the same instruments as open procedures
rationale: Laparoscopic sets require specialized instruments including trocars, laparoscopic graspers, scissors, clip appliers, ESU accessories, and camera/light systems.

stem: What is a Weitlaner retractor and when is it commonly used?
options: A self-retaining retractor with sharp or blunt prongs used for superficial tissue retraction in smaller surgical sites
A hand-held retractor used exclusively for deep abdominal surgery
A ribbon retractor for liver retraction
A malleable retractor that can be bent to shape
rationale: The Weitlaner is a self-retaining retractor with sharp or blunt prongs, commonly used for superficial tissue retraction in smaller surgical exposures.

stem: What special supplies must the surgical technologist prepare for a VP (ventriculoperitoneal) shunt placement?
options: VP shunt system (ventricular catheter, valve, distal catheter), tunneling device, reservoir, and sterile saline for flushing
Standard general surgery supplies only
Only intravenous tubing and a drainage bag
Orthopedic screws and plates for skull fixation
rationale: VP shunt placement requires the complete shunt system, tunneling device, reservoir, and flushing supplies for proper CSF diversion.

stem: What is the surgical technologist's role during a cystoscopy procedure?
options: Prepare the cystoscopy instruments including telescope, sheath, obturator, and light cord; connect irrigation; and assist with biopsy instruments as needed
Only hand the cystoscope to the surgeon
Manage only the irrigation system
Cystoscopy does not require a surgical technologist
rationale: During cystoscopy, the ST prepares all instruments, connects irrigation, and assists with biopsy instruments and specimen collection.

stem: What physiological effects does the Trendelenburg position have on the patient?
options: Increased venous return to the heart, increased intracranial pressure, cephalad shift of abdominal organs, and potential respiratory compromise from diaphragmatic pressure
Decreased blood flow to the brain and improved respiratory function
No significant physiological changes occur
Only affects blood pressure without other systemic effects
rationale: Trendelenburg causes increased venous return, raised ICP, cephalad organ shift, and respiratory compromise from diaphragmatic displacement.

stem: What is the maximum recommended time a patient should remain in the lithotomy position to minimize compartment syndrome risk?
options: Generally limited to less than 4 hours with periodic assessment of the legs; lower extremity perfusion should be checked at regular intervals
There is no time limit as long as the patient is padded
8 hours maximum for any patient
2 hours maximum regardless of padding or monitoring
rationale: Extended lithotomy positioning (>4 hours) significantly increases compartment syndrome risk; periodic leg assessment and time limitation are essential.

stem: What is the most effective method of surgical site skin preparation?
options: Chlorhexidine gluconate-alcohol combination applied in a back-and-forth friction scrub pattern, allowed to dry completely
Povidone-iodine paint applied once without drying time
Soap and water wash at the surgical site
No skin preparation is needed for clean procedures
rationale: Chlorhexidine-alcohol combination provides the most effective surgical site antisepsis when applied with friction and allowed to dry completely.

stem: What is the contact time requirement for high-level disinfection with glutaraldehyde?
options: A minimum of 20 minutes at the proper concentration, followed by thorough rinsing with sterile water
5 minutes of soaking is sufficient
60 seconds of exposure
No specific contact time is required
rationale: Glutaraldehyde requires a minimum 20-minute contact time at proper concentration for high-level disinfection, followed by sterile water rinsing.

stem: What position is typically used for administering spinal anesthesia?
options: Lateral decubitus or sitting position with the patient's back curved to open the intervertebral spaces
Supine position with the head elevated
Prone position with a pillow under the abdomen
Standing position for the fastest onset
rationale: Spinal anesthesia is administered in the lateral decubitus or sitting position with spinal flexion to maximize intervertebral space opening.

stem: What is the surgical technologist's role during a procedure performed under local anesthesia with monitored anesthesia care (MAC)?
options: Maintain the sterile field, provide instruments, and be prepared for potential conversion to general anesthesia if needed
MAC cases do not require a surgical technologist
Only monitor the patient's vital signs
The ST leaves the room during MAC cases
rationale: During MAC cases, the ST maintains the sterile field, assists with instruments, and stays prepared for potential conversion to general anesthesia.

stem: What should the surgical technologist prepare immediately when laryngospasm is reported by the anesthesia team?
options: Prepare for possible emergency tracheotomy by having a tracheotomy tray available and ensuring all airway management supplies are accessible
Continue with the procedure without concern
Have only suction available
Prepare for cardiac surgery
rationale: Laryngospasm may require emergency tracheotomy; the ST must have a tracheotomy tray and airway management supplies immediately accessible.

stem: What is the difference between electrocautery (monopolar) and bipolar electrosurgery in controlling hemorrhage?
options: Monopolar passes current through the patient to a return pad and is used for cutting and coagulation, while bipolar confines current between the instrument tips and is safer near delicate structures
There is no difference between the two methods
Bipolar is used only for cutting while monopolar is only for coagulation
Monopolar is always safer than bipolar
rationale: Monopolar current travels through the patient requiring a return pad, while bipolar confines energy between the tips, making it safer for delicate tissue hemostasis.

stem: What should occur if a discrepancy is identified during the surgical timeout?
options: The procedure must be paused until the discrepancy is resolved and all team members are in agreement
Proceed with the surgery and document the discrepancy afterward
Only the surgeon can decide whether to proceed despite the discrepancy
Discrepancies during timeouts are handled by risk management later
rationale: Any discrepancy during the surgical timeout requires the procedure to stop until resolution and team consensus are achieved.

stem: What is the surgical technologist's responsibility regarding medications on the sterile field?
options: All medications and solutions must be labeled with drug name and concentration, verified with the circulator at the time of transfer, and reconfirmed before use
Only antibiotics need labeling on the sterile field
Labeling is optional if only one medication is being used
The surgeon is solely responsible for medication management on the sterile field
rationale: All medications and solutions on the sterile field must be labeled, verified at transfer, and confirmed before use to prevent medication errors.
